1140 if method == "common": 1141 return round(value, precision) 1142 1143 func = getattr(math, method) 1144 return t.cast(float, func(value * (10**precision)) / (10**precision)) 1145 1146 1147 class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): 1148 grouper: t.Any 1149 list: t.List 1150 1151 # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print 1152 # out the value during debugging. 1153 def __repr__(self) -> str: 1154 return tuple.__repr__(self) 1155 1156 def __str__(self) -> str: 1157 return tuple.__str__(self) 1158 1159 1160 @pass_environment 1161 def sync_do_groupby( 1162 environment: "Environment", 1163 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1164 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1165 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1166 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1167 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's 1168 :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}

"""Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's
:func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for

input.old_string:
.. versionchanged:: 3.0
        Added the ``default`` parameter.

    .. versionchanged:: 2.6
        The attribute supports dot notation for nested access.
    """
    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    return [
        _GroupTuple(key, list(values))
        for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr)
    ]


@async_variant(sync_do_groupby)  # type: ignore
async def do_groupby(
    environment: "Environment",
    value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]",
    attribute: t.Union[str, int],
    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]":
    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    return [
        _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values))
        for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr)
    ]
input.new_string:
You can also set ``with_counts`` to ``True`` to include the number of items in each group, without having to count the items yourself with ``|list|length``. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if with_counts: out = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr): values = list(values) out.append(_GroupTupleWithCount(key, values, len(values))) return out return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if with_counts: out = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr): values = await auto_to_list(values) out.append(_GroupTupleWithCount(key, values, len(values))) return out return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]

content.text: Now let's check the async `groupby` values type — it's a regular iterator from itertools.groupby (sync), so `auto_to_list` should handle plain iterables fine. Let's verify with a quick test.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}')
users = [
    {'name': 'a', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'b', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'c', 'city': 'LA'},
]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.text: Both work. Now let's test the async variant.

input.command:
python -c "
import asyncio
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment(enable_async=True)
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}')
users = [
    {'name': 'a', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'b', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'c', 'city': 'LA'},
]
print(asyncio.run(tmpl.render_async(users=users)))
"

content.text: Now let's run the existing groupby tests to make sure nothing broke.

content.text:
The patch is complete and verified. Summary:

- Added `_GroupTupleWithCount` NamedTuple (`grouper`, `list`, `count`) alongside the existing `_GroupTuple`.
- Added `with_counts: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` (async variant) in `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
- When `with_counts=True`, the filter yields `(grouper, items, count)` tuples; default behavior (`with_counts=False`) is unchanged for backward compatibility.
- Updated the docstring with an example and versionchanged note.
- Verified with manual sync/async template renders and the existing `groupby` test suite (all passing).

I also broadcast a `STATUS` message to agent1, who claimed the same function for a `case_sensitive` parameter, to flag the overlap since both of us are modifying `sync_do_groupby`/`do_groupby`. Final diff is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`.

result:
The patch is complete and verified. Summary:

- Added `_GroupTupleWithCount` NamedTuple (`grouper`, `list`, `count`) alongside the existing `_GroupTuple`.
- Added `with_counts: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` (async variant) in `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
- When `with_counts=True`, the filter yields `(grouper, items, count)` tuples; default behavior (`with_counts=False`) is unchanged for backward compatibility.
- Updated the docstring with an example and versionchanged note.
- Verified with manual sync/async template renders and the existing `groupby` test suite (all passing).

I also broadcast a `STATUS` message to agent1, who claimed the same function for a `case_sensitive` parameter, to flag the overlap since both of us are modifying `sync_do_groupby`/`do_groupby`. Final diff is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`.
